steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim is that the banker is "very upset about something." A supporting quotation must show the banker experiencing emotional distress — not just physical action, atmospheric setting, or a contradicting emotion.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "Then the banker cautiously broke the seals off the door and put the key in the keyhole.": This describes a deliberate physical action. There is no emotion or distress shown — the banker is simply doing something carefully. Does not illustrate being upset. ✗
  • Choice B — "It struck three o'clock, the banker listened; everyone was asleep in the house and nothing could be heard outside but the rustling of the chilled trees.": This sets an atmospheric scene. It conveys quiet and tension but shows no emotional distress from the banker himself. ✗
  • Choice C — "The banker, spoilt and frivolous, with millions beyond his reckoning, was delighted at the bet.": This directly contradicts the claim. "Delighted" is the opposite of upset. ✗
  • Choice D — "When [the banker] got home he lay on his bed, but his tears and emotion kept him for hours from sleeping.": Tears and uncontrollable emotion preventing sleep is direct, concrete evidence of being very upset. This is the only choice that shows the banker's internal emotional state matching the claim. ✓
That is the correct answer.
